episodes.title: Bulls on Course, Valley of the Deals, and CNBC Investigates 7/7/26
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
A broad US markets show covering a tech-led equity pullback (Samsung earnings, SpaceX's NASDAQ 100 inclusion), Amazon's $25bn bond raise, Sun Valley media deal chatter, Walmart grocery price cuts, and a CNBC investigation into blacklisted Chinese LiDAR maker HSI. Most direct relevance to an EM trader comes through Fed/rate-cut expectations tied to falling oil and softening CPI, and continued mega-cap credit issuance signaling tighter corporate liquidity dynamics.

**Key points**
- Nasdaq/semis fell ~5% in 3 of the last 4 sessions after Samsung's profit jump disappointed elevated expectations; SOX index back to mid-June levels after a +90% YTD run.
- Bespoke's Paul Hickey: doesn't expect the Fed to hike or cut before year-end, but says inflation appears to have peaked — crude oil's two-month decline and a falling ISM prices-paid index point to a flat-to-negative month-over-month CPI in next week's report.
- Walmart is rolling out summer grocery price cuts (ground beef -12%, private label and Coca-Cola/PepsiCo/Frito-Lay items) after its worst quarter in four years; Kroger testing similar cuts.
- Amazon is raising $25bn in bonds for AI/data-center capex, joining Google, Oracle, Meta and SpaceX in heavy tech bond issuance this year; Amazon expects negative free cash flow in 2026.
- CNBC investigation: Shanghai-based HSI, blacklisted by the Pentagon for alleged Chinese military ties, still supplies LiDAR sensors to Nvidia/Amazon Zoox-linked autonomous platforms at roughly half the cost of US competitors, raising data-security concerns given China's legal power to compel data access.
- US housing market survey shows buyer leverage rising (44% of agents report balanced market vs 30% a year ago); Sunbelt markets softening.

**FX / rates implications**
- Softer oil and a potentially cooler US CPI print reduce the odds of Fed hawkish surprises, supportive for broad EM carry (INR, IDR, PHP, MXN, BRL) via lower UST yields and a steadier-to-softer DXY.
- Falling oil is a tailwind for oil-importing EM Asia currencies (INR, IDR, PHP) via improved terms of trade and current-account dynamics, while a negative for LATAM oil exporters' (COP, MXN via Pemex-adjacent flows) terms of trade.
- Heavy US mega-cap bond issuance (Amazon, Google, Oracle, Meta, SpaceX) could tighten global credit conditions and compete for dollar liquidity, a mild headwind for EM credit spreads and hard-currency sovereign issuance.
- US-China tech/security friction (HSI blacklist scrutiny) keeps trade and tech-decoupling risk alive, a background negative for CNY/CNH and broader Asia FX risk sentiment.

**Watch list**
- Next week's US CPI print — key catalyst for Fed rate-cut repricing and UST yields/DXY direction.
- PepsiCo earnings Thursday for read-through on consumer/grocery pricing and input-cost trends.
- Further mega-cap bond issuance and its effect on credit spreads and dollar funding costs.
- SpaceX share-lockup expirations and Russell/NASDAQ rebalancing flows as a gauge of broader risk appetite.

episodes.title: South Sea Bubble: the shoemaker’s son who sparked Britain’s first financial crisis
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
This episode of Behind the Money recounts the South Sea Bubble of 1720, Britain's first stock market crash, framing it as fundamentally a story about government debt management rather than trade. It has no direct bearing on current EM FX/rates markets but offers a historical case study in bubble mechanics, debt-for-equity conversions, and market psychology that recurs across financial cycles.

**Key points**
- The South Sea Company was created in 1711 as a debt-for-equity swap vehicle to consolidate Britain's messy, high-interest government debt (~£9m, later expanded to £30m in 1720) into tradable shares.
- In the 1720 restructuring, share exchange prices were allowed to float with the market rather than fixed at par, letting the company mint new shares at inflated prices and pump its own stock.
- Shares rose from ~£100-130 to a peak of £1,050 in June 1720 before collapsing to £120 by December, wiping out investors including Isaac Newton and members of the political establishment; the debt was only fully repaid by Britain in 2015.
- Architect John Blunt, a shoemaker's son, personally profited (~£300,000, roughly £165m today) before being stripped of most of his fortune after the crash, while widespread bribery of politicians and courtiers enabled the scheme.
- Guest Thomas Levenson draws parallels to the 2008 subprime crisis, arguing bubbles stem from good financial innovations layered with excess leverage and euphoria rather than being intrinsic to capitalism itself.

**FX / rates implications**
None specific to current EM Asia or LATAM markets — this is a historical/educational segment with no read-through for USD, Fed policy, or EM currencies today.

**Watch list**
- Next week's follow-up episode will cover Robert Walpole's cleanup of the South Sea Bubble aftermath and the birth of the UK premiership.

episodes.title: Cut to the Chase! The summer market checklist
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Standard Chartered's Daniel Lam runs through a summer market checklist focused on US equities, framing several catalysts that will shape global risk sentiment ahead of the next Fed policy move — a key channel for EM FX and rates even though the episode does not name individual EM markets.

**Key points**
- The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) posted its worst two-day drop versus the S&P 500 in at least five years last Wednesday and Thursday.
- Historically, SOX underperformance versus the S&P 500 has been a buying opportunity, typically recovering lost ground within a month; failure to do so this time could signal a more systemic shift in market perception of AI development.
- Earnings season will be scrutinised for hyperscaler capex guidance — capex upside could pressure hyperscaler share prices short term but would support the semiconductor supply chain.
- US equity positioning remains crowded and needs to move toward neutral territory for markets to build a solid bottom.
- Markets are pricing a Fed hike expected in either September or October; historically (barring 2022) the S&P 500 has risen into the first hike of a cycle and declined afterward.
- Retail investor appetite for buying US equities is abnormally low currently, though retail remains active in options (calls and puts); a pickup in bullish retail flow could be a major sentiment booster.

**FX / rates implications**
- Broad risk sentiment tied to US equity positioning and the SOX/AI-narrative is a key swing factor for EM FX carry trades and risk appetite generally.
- Fed policy timing (hike priced for September/October) is a direct driver of USD trajectory and, in turn, EM Asia and LATAM currency and rates positioning.
- A move to less-crowded, more neutral equity positioning could reduce volatility spillover into EM risk assets.

**Watch list**
- SOX performance versus S&P 500 over the coming month — whether the historical bounce-back pattern holds.
- Hyperscaler earnings and capex commentary this earnings season.
- Fed policy signals ahead of the September/October meeting window.
- Retail options flow (calls vs puts) for signs of a bullish sentiment pickup.
